candidates.rationale: RESTORE Act construction grants are geographically restricted to Gulf Coast states, making this a poor fit for an Arizona-based firm with no indicated Gulf Coast presence.

steps.message: Drafting for PWEAA2023 — best uncertain match in shortlist.

result.summary: This application skeleton is intentionally minimal because the opportunity record (PWEAA2023) contains no description, eligibility text, funding range, award count, or close date. Before drafting any section, the applicant must retrieve the full Notice of Funding Opportunity (NOFO) for PWEAA2023 from Grants.gov, SAM.gov, or the issuing agency's website. The prompts below are diagnostic placeholders to gather the information needed to build a compliant skeleton once the NOFO is in hand.

sections.heading: PRE-WORK: Retrieve and Validate the Full NOFO
sections.guidance: No compliant skeleton can be built without the actual solicitation. This section is a checklist to ensure the applicant has the authoritative source document before spending time on narrative sections.
promptsForApplicant: Have you located the full NOFO for PWEAA2023 on Grants.gov or SAM.gov? If yes, what is the exact title, issuing agency, CFDA/Assistance Listing number, and close date?
What is the stated funding range per award and the total program funding pool?
Does the NOFO specify a required submission structure (e.g., named sections, page limits, attachments)? List each required section heading exactly as it appears.
What file formats and submission portal does the NOFO require (Grants.gov Workspace, agency-specific portal, email, etc.)?
Is there a pre-application, letter of intent, or registration deadline that precedes the full application close date?

sections.heading: Eligibility Confirmation
sections.guidance: For-profit general contractors (NAICS 236220) are frequently ineligible for federal grants unless the NOFO explicitly permits for-profit entities. This section must document every eligibility criterion and confirm the applicant meets each one before any further work is done.
promptsForApplicant: Does the NOFO explicitly list for-profit businesses or commercial contractors as eligible applicants?
Does the NOFO impose a NAICS code restriction? If so, is 236220 (Commercial and Institutional Building Construction) listed or excluded?
Does the NOFO require any socioeconomic status (8(a), HUBZone, WOSB, SDVOSB, disadvantaged business)? If so, does this applicant hold any of those certifications?
Does the NOFO impose a minimum or maximum years-in-operation requirement that a 6-year-old firm must meet?
Does the NOFO impose a geographic restriction (state, region, project service area) that affects an Arizona-based entity working in the Phoenix metro?
Does the NOFO require active SAM.gov registration? Is the applicant's SAM.gov registration current and not expired?

sections.heading: Statement of Need
sections.guidance: Once the NOFO is confirmed, this section must align the applicant's work with the problem the grant program is designed to solve. Use data specific to the Phoenix metro and the infrastructure sector the program targets.
promptsForApplicant: What specific infrastructure problem or community need does the PWEAA2023 program aim to address, according to the NOFO?
What local or regional data (Phoenix metro, Maricopa County, AZ statewide) quantifies the gap or need this project would address?
What populations or jurisdictions would benefit from the proposed work, and why does the Phoenix metro geography make this applicant's scope relevant?
How does the need described in the NOFO connect directly to the type of commercial infrastructure work this firm performs?

sections.heading: Project Approach
sections.guidance: Must demonstrate a technically sound, feasible work plan that maps to the NOFO's program objectives and any prescribed activities or deliverables. A 12-person firm must show it can execute at the scale the grant requires.
promptsForApplicant: What specific activities, construction scopes, or services will this project deliver, and how do they match the NOFO's eligible use of funds?
What is the proposed project timeline, and how does it fit within the period of performance stated in the NOFO?
How will a firm with 12 employees and $2.4M annual revenue manage the project workload — will subcontractors, teaming partners, or additional hires be required?
What past projects of similar type, scale, and complexity has this firm completed that demonstrate technical capability for this work?
Does the NOFO require specific construction standards, environmental reviews, prevailing wage compliance (Davis-Bacon), or Buy America/Build America Act provisions? How will the firm comply?

sections.heading: Organizational Capability
sections.guidance: Reviewers will scrutinize whether a small, for-profit contractor has the administrative infrastructure to manage federal funds, comply with reporting requirements, and complete the project. Six years of operation and a $2.4M revenue base must be positioned as sufficient.
promptsForApplicant: Has this firm previously held a federal contract or grant? If yes, list the award number, agency, and dollar value.
Who on staff will serve as project manager and grants administrator, and what are their relevant credentials?
Does the firm have an accounting system capable of tracking federal expenditures separately and producing required financial reports (e.g., SF-425)?
What licenses, bonding, and insurance does the firm currently hold, and do they meet the NOFO's or the project's requirements?
Does the NOFO require indirect cost rate documentation? Does the firm have a negotiated indirect cost rate agreement (NICRA), or will it use the de minimis 10% MTDC rate?

sections.heading: Budget Narrative
sections.guidance: Every line item must be allowable, allocable, and reasonable under 2 CFR Part 200 (Uniform Guidance) if this is a federal assistance award. The budget must be sized within the NOFO's funding range, which is currently unknown.
promptsForApplicant: What is the total requested amount, and does it fall within the NOFO's stated minimum and maximum award size?
What are the major cost categories (labor, materials, subcontracts, equipment, overhead), and what is the dollar amount and justification for each?
Does the NOFO require a cost share or match? If so, what is the percentage, and what non-federal resources will the firm commit?
Are any proposed costs explicitly unallowable under 2 CFR Part 200 or the NOFO's specific restrictions (e.g., profit/fee on grant-funded work, entertainment, lobbying)?
How were labor rates determined — are they consistent with the firm's existing payroll records and, if applicable, Davis-Bacon wage determinations for the project location?

sections.heading: Outcomes & Evaluation
sections.guidance: Federal grant reviewers expect measurable, time-bound outcomes tied to the program's stated goals and a credible plan for tracking and reporting them.
promptsForApplicant: What specific, measurable outputs does the NOFO require applicants to report (e.g., square footage built, jobs created, infrastructure units installed)?
What are this project's 1-year and end-of-grant performance targets for each required metric?
How will data be collected, who is responsible, and how will it be reported to the agency on the schedule the NOFO requires?
If the project does not meet its performance targets, what is the firm's contingency plan, and does the NOFO specify consequences for underperformance?

watchOuts: CRITICAL — For-profit eligibility: Federal grants routinely exclude for-profit businesses. Because the NOFO text is absent, this is the single highest-risk disqualifier. Do not invest further proposal effort until for-profit eligibility is confirmed in writing from the NOFO.

Missing NOFO: PWEAA2023 returned no description, agency, funding range, or close date. The opportunity may be expired, miskeyed, or agency-internal. Verify the solicitation number is correct before proceeding.

SAM.gov registration: Federal assistance awards require active SAM.gov registration at time of application and throughout the award. Confirm the firm's registration is current — registrations expire annually.
No socioeconomic certifications: This firm holds no 8(a), HUBZone, WOSB, SDVOSB, or SDB designations. If the NOFO is set aside for any of these categories, the firm is ineligible.

Indirect cost rate: A 12-person firm likely lacks a NICRA. Without one, the firm is limited to the de minimis 10% of Modified Total Direct Costs. If the budget relies on a higher indirect rate, that rate must be negotiated with the cognizant federal agency before award.

Davis-Bacon Act: Construction-related federal assistance awards almost always trigger prevailing wage requirements. If the firm's labor cost estimates do not incorporate current Davis-Bacon wage determinations for Maricopa County, the budget will be noncompliant.

Buy America / Build America Act: Infrastructure grants funded through BIL or similar legislation require use of American-made iron, steel, manufactured products, and construction materials. Confirm applicability and supply-chain compliance before committing to a cost or schedule.

Revenue-to-award-size ratio: At $2.4M annual revenue, the firm may not meet bonding capacity or financial responsibility thresholds if the award is large relative to its balance sheet. Check the NOFO for minimum financial capacity requirements.

Close date unknown: With no close date in the record, there is risk the opportunity is already closed. Confirm current status immediately on Grants.gov before allocating staff time to this application.

steps.message: Drafting for 2026-NIST-PMGP-01 — best uncertain match in shortlist.

result.summary: A veteran-owned precision machining startup (NAICS 332710) in northeast Ohio with 1.5 years of operation and $420K revenue seeks federal manufacturing grant support. The proposal angle should emphasize domestic aerospace/defense supply chain strengthening, veteran entrepreneurship, and workforce-ready advanced manufacturing capacity-building. Because the specific opportunity (2026-NIST-PMGP-01) has no published description, eligibility text, or funding range, this skeleton is built on NIST Manufacturing Extension Partnership and Manufacturing USA program conventions — applicant must verify actual FOA requirements before submitting.

sections.heading: Executive Summary / Cover Abstract
sections.guidance: Provide reviewers an at-a-glance statement of who you are, what you will do with the funds, and the quantifiable impact. NIST-adjacent programs typically expect this to map directly to program priorities such as supply chain resilience, domestic manufacturing competitiveness, and workforce development.
promptsForApplicant: In one paragraph, what specific manufacturing capability gap will this grant address, and how does closing that gap benefit U.S. aerospace/defense supply chains?
What is the single most compelling metric you can promise (jobs created, parts domestically sourced, revenue growth) and over what time horizon?
How does your veteran-owned status connect to the mission or community impact of this project?

sections.heading: Statement of Need
sections.guidance: Establish the problem at the market/industry level before narrowing to your firm. Reviewers must see that the need is real, documented, and that your company is positioned at the center of it. Cite publicly available data on aerospace/defense supply chain vulnerabilities, domestic machining capacity shortfalls, or regional manufacturing workforce gaps in northeast Ohio.
promptsForApplicant: What documented supply chain vulnerability or domestic sourcing gap exists among your current aerospace/defense customers that your expansion would address?
What third-party data (BLS, NAM, Ohio MEP, OEMS, customer letters) can you cite to validate demand for precision machining capacity in the Cleveland/northeast Ohio corridor?
Why is a 1.5-year-old startup — rather than an established incumbent — the right entity to fill this gap right now?
Are any of your current customers prime contractors or Tier 1 defense suppliers? Can you obtain letters of support or intent to purchase?

sections.heading: Project Approach / Technical Plan
sections.guidance: Describe the specific activities, milestones, and technical methods you will use to achieve the project goals. NIST manufacturing programs expect a credible, time-phased work plan with discrete deliverables. Vague plans are a primary scoring weakness for early-stage firms.
promptsForApplicant: What specific equipment, technology, or process capability will grant funds be used to acquire or develop (make, model, lead time, vendor quotes)?
What is your 12- to 24-month milestone schedule — list at least five concrete, date-bound deliverables?
How will you achieve and verify precision tolerances or quality certifications (e.g., AS9100D, ITAR registration, CMMC) required by aerospace/defense customers?
What is your production ramp plan — how many parts or contracts can you take on after the grant-funded capability is operational?
Will you partner with any Ohio MEP center, community college, or defense industry consortium as part of the project approach?

sections.heading: Organizational Capability
sections.guidance: Reviewers will scrutinize a 1.5-year-old, 5-person firm carefully. You must demonstrate that despite limited operating history, the team has the expertise, facilities, and management systems to execute a federally funded project and comply with federal award requirements. Veteran ownership should be highlighted as a programmatic asset, not just a demographic checkbox.
promptsForApplicant: What are the prior professional credentials, years of machining experience, and relevant certifications of the owner(s) and key personnel before this company was founded?
What quality management systems or certifications does the company currently hold or have in-process (ISO 9001, AS9100, ITAR)?
Do you have an active SAM.gov registration and a CAGE code? If not, what is your plan to obtain them before award?
What is your current facility footprint (sq ft, owned vs. leased, zoning), and is it sufficient to support the expanded capacity described in the project?
Who will serve as the day-to-day federal grants manager, and what experience does that person have with federal compliance (2 CFR 200, reporting, audits)?

sections.heading: Budget Narrative
sections.guidance: Every line must be allowable, allocable, and reasonable under 2 CFR 200 (Uniform Guidance). For a manufacturing grant, the primary cost categories are typically equipment, labor, indirect costs, and any subawards. Reviewers will look for cost-share commitments and will question any budget that appears to fund normal business operations rather than a discrete project.
promptsForApplicant: What is your total project cost, and how much are you requesting from the federal government versus contributing as cost share (cash or in-kind)?
Can you provide vendor quotes or published list prices for each major equipment line item to justify the cost as reasonable?
Which labor costs (hours × rate) are directly attributable to the project versus ongoing business operations, and how will you track and document that distinction?
Do you have an approved indirect cost rate, or will you use the 10% de minimis rate under 2 CFR 200.414(f)?
If you are purchasing capital equipment, how will you handle title, disposition, and depreciation per federal property standards after the award period ends?

sections.heading: Outcomes & Evaluation
sections.guidance: Federal reviewers expect measurable, time-bound outcomes tied to program priorities. NIST manufacturing programs typically prioritize jobs, economic output, supply chain resilience, and technology adoption metrics. You must also specify how you will collect data and report progress.
promptsForApplicant: What are your three to five primary performance metrics, and what is the baseline value of each today?
How many net new full-time-equivalent jobs do you project creating, in what roles, and by what date?
What increase in annual revenue or new contracts do you project as a direct result of this grant, and what assumptions underlie that projection?
How will you collect and verify outcome data (customer POs, payroll records, tax filings), and who is responsible for maintaining that documentation?
If you fall short of a projected outcome, what is your corrective action plan, and at what threshold would you notify the program officer?

watchOuts: CRITICAL — No FOA published: The opportunity description and eligibility text are entirely blank. Before investing further in this application, you must locate and download the actual 2026-NIST-PMGP-01 Notice of Funding Opportunity from Grants.gov or NIST.gov. Everything in this skeleton may conflict with the real requirements.

Years in operation risk: Many NIST and federal manufacturing programs require a minimum of 2–3 years of operating history. At 1.5 years, you may be ineligible. Confirm the FOA's minimum operational history requirement immediately.

SAM.gov registration: For-profit entities must have an active SAM.gov registration with a current Unique Entity Identifier (UEI) before an award can be made. Confirm your registration is active and not expiring near the close date.

NAICS code alignment: Confirm that NAICS 332710 (Machine Shops) is listed as an eligible NAICS code in the actual FOA. Some manufacturing programs restrict eligibility to specific NAICS ranges or exclude pure job-shop operations.

For-profit entity restrictions: Some NIST programs (particularly those under Manufacturing USA) limit direct awards to non-profit institutes or consortia, with for-profits participating only as subawardees or industry partners. Verify the for-profit direct applicant pathway is open.

Cost-share mandate: NIST programs frequently require 1:1 or greater non-federal cost share. A company with $420K annual revenue must demonstrate it can credibly commit and document cost share without jeopardizing operations.

ITAR/export control: Aerospace and defense machining work may trigger ITAR registration requirements. Unregistered ITAR status could be a compliance red flag for reviewers and potentially disqualify certain activities.

CMMC readiness: If any project work touches Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) from defense customers, Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification (CMMC) compliance may be required as a condition of award or subaward.

Funding range unknown: With no published funding floor or ceiling, you cannot size your budget request appropriately. Submitting an out-of-range budget (too small to be competitive or too large relative to award size) is a common disqualifier.
Close date unknown: Without a confirmed close date, you risk missing mandatory pre-submission steps such as SAM.gov renewal, letters of support gathering, or mandatory pre-application webinars.

result.summary: A Phoenix-based commercial general contractor with 6 years of operation and $2.4M in annual revenue seeks federal infrastructure funding under PWEAA2023. The proposal must establish the firm's technical capacity for small-to-mid commercial construction projects and articulate a clear public works or community infrastructure benefit. Because nearly all eligibility and structural details for this opportunity are unknown, the skeleton below defaults to the standard five-section federal grant structure and flags the significant data gaps that must be resolved before submission.

sections.heading: Statement of Need
sections.guidance: Establish the specific infrastructure gap or public works deficiency the project will address in the Phoenix metro area. Because the grant description is absent, the need must be framed broadly enough to adapt once the actual program priorities are confirmed, but specifically enough to demonstrate local market knowledge.
promptsForApplicant: What specific infrastructure problem or service gap in the Phoenix metro area is this project designed to solve, and what data (e.g., city plans, utility reports, growth projections) supports the existence of that gap?
Who are the primary beneficiaries of the proposed project — residents, businesses, government entities — and how many people or entities will be directly affected?
Why is federal funding necessary for this project rather than private financing or local/state public funds?
What happens if this project is NOT funded — what is the consequence of inaction for the community or infrastructure system?

sections.heading: Project Approach
sections.guidance: Describe the scope of construction or infrastructure work to be performed, the methodology, timeline, and how the firm's NAICS 236220 (commercial building construction) capabilities align with the work. Reviewers will look for a credible, phased work plan with measurable milestones.
promptsForApplicant: What is the precise scope of work — what will be built, renovated, or improved, and at what address or site?
What is the project timeline from contract award through construction completion, broken into discrete phases with start and end dates?
Which trade disciplines will be performed in-house by your 12 employees versus subcontracted, and do you have identified, qualified subcontractors?
How does your prior project portfolio (past 6 years) demonstrate that your firm has successfully delivered comparable work in terms of scale, complexity, and budget?
What permits, environmental reviews, or site control documents are already in place or in process for this project?

sections.heading: Organizational Capability
sections.guidance: Demonstrate that a 12-person, for-profit GC with $2.4M annual revenue has the administrative, financial, and technical capacity to manage a federal grant award. Reviewers will scrutinize whether the firm can handle federal compliance requirements (Davis-Bacon, SAM.gov registration, reporting) at its current staff size.
promptsForApplicant: Is your entity currently registered and active in SAM.gov, and what is your UEI number?
Who on your staff (by name and role) will serve as Project Manager and Grant Administrator, and what federal grant management experience do they have?
Can you provide three comparable completed projects — with owner contact references — that demonstrate relevant commercial infrastructure work in the $500K–$3M range?
What accounting system do you use, and has it been audited or reviewed by an independent CPA? Can it track grant expenditures separately from operating funds?
Do you currently carry the insurance coverages (general liability, workers' comp, professional liability) typically required by federal construction awards, and at what limits?

sections.heading: Budget Narrative
sections.guidance: Provide a line-item budget that justifies all requested costs as allowable, allocable, and reasonable under 2 CFR Part 200 (Uniform Guidance). Because the funding range for PWEAA2023 is unknown, prepare a scalable budget and clearly separate federal request from any match or cost-share.
promptsForApplicant: What is your total project cost estimate, broken down by labor, materials, equipment, subcontracts, indirect/overhead, and other direct costs?
Are any of your cost elements based on executed or executed-pending bids, or are they estimates — and what documentation supports each estimate?
What is your proposed federal ask versus any non-federal match, and what is the source and commitment status of the match funds?
What is your indirect cost rate — do you have a federally negotiated indirect cost rate agreement (NICRA), or will you use the 10% de minimis rate under 2 CFR §200.414(f)?
How will you prevent commingling of federal grant funds with your firm's operating accounts, and who has signature authority over grant expenditures?

sections.heading: Outcomes & Evaluation
sections.guidance: Define measurable outputs and outcomes the project will produce, and describe how progress will be tracked and reported. Federal reviewers expect SMART metrics tied to the stated need, not just construction completion.
promptsForApplicant: What are the 3–5 primary quantifiable outputs of the project (e.g., square footage built, linear feet of infrastructure installed, number of jobs created or retained)?
What longer-term outcomes — beyond project completion — will demonstrate that the infrastructure gap identified in your Statement of Need has been meaningfully addressed?
How will you collect and verify performance data during the project, and who is responsible for that data collection?
What is your reporting cadence for the federal grantor (quarterly, semi-annual), and what system or tool will you use to produce those reports?
If the project falls behind schedule or over budget, what is your contingency plan and at what threshold would you notify the federal program officer?

watchOuts: CRITICAL — Opportunity details are almost entirely unknown (no agency, no funding range, no award count, no close date, no eligibility text, no description). You must obtain the full NOFO for PWEAA2023 before drafting any prose; every section of this skeleton may need to be restructured once actual submission requirements are confirmed.

For-profit entity status is a disqualifier for many federal grant programs — confirm explicitly in the NOFO that for-profit contractors are eligible; many public works grants restrict awards to units of government, nonprofits, or tribal entities.

No socioeconomic set-aside certifications (8(a), WOSB, SDVOSB, HUBZone) are held by this firm, which means the entity cannot benefit from preference programs and must compete in the full-and-open pool — confirm the NOFO does not require a certification your firm lacks.

With $2.4M annual revenue and 12 employees, the firm may fall below minimum bonding or surety capacity thresholds that federal construction grants sometimes require (performance and payment bonds on federally funded work).
Davis-Bacon Act prevailing wage requirements almost certainly apply to any federally funded construction work — confirm your payroll systems and subcontract templates are compliant before submission.
SAM.gov registration must be active at time of application and award; a lapsed or pending registration is an automatic administrative rejection at many agencies.
6 years in operation may be below a minimum years-in-business threshold if one is stated in the actual NOFO — verify once the full opportunity document is obtained.

Arizona zip code 85004 is in Maricopa County; some public works grants require the project site to be in a designated distressed community, rural area, or qualified opportunity zone — confirm the project site qualifies under any geographic eligibility criteria in the actual NOFO.

steps.message: Drafting for HRSA-26-036 — best uncertain match in shortlist.

result.summary: A 4-year-old Vermont nonprofit delivering trade-skills training to under-employed adults seeks HRSA-26-036 funding. Because no description or eligibility text was supplied, the skeleton below is built on HRSA's standard workforce-development and training grant structure; applicant must verify actual NOFO sections before use. The proposal angle should emphasize rural workforce gaps in central Vermont, the organization's demonstrated community reach despite small size, and its disadvantaged-business/community-serving status.

sections.heading: Project Abstract / Executive Summary
sections.guidance: Provide a concise snapshot (typically 1 page or fewer) that reviewers use to route and score the application. Must name the target population, intervention, geography, and anticipated outcomes. Because HRSA funding ranges and award counts are unconfirmed, do not cite dollar figures here until the official NOFO is located.
promptsForApplicant: In one sentence, what specific workforce problem does your organization solve, and for whom?
What is the single most measurable outcome you expect to achieve with this grant (e.g., number of completers, credentials earned, job placements)?
Which HRSA priority areas or health-workforce shortage categories does your trade-skills training address, if any?
What is the total funding amount you plan to request, and over what project period?

sections.heading: Statement of Need
sections.guidance: Establish the documented workforce and economic gap in central Vermont that justifies federal investment. HRSA grants typically require data-driven need statements tied to underserved or rural populations. Reviewers will look for local labor-market evidence, not just national statistics.
promptsForApplicant: What Vermont Department of Labor or U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics data can you cite to quantify under-employment in your service area (Washington County and surrounding counties)?
What trades are most in shortage in central Vermont, and what is your source for that claim?
How many under-employed adults currently lack access to the training you provide within a reasonable commute of ZIP 05641?
What barriers (cost, schedule, transportation, prior education requirements) prevent this population from accessing existing programs, and how do you know?
How does your organization's 4-year track record demonstrate unmet need that persists despite your current capacity?

sections.heading: Project Approach / Work Plan
sections.guidance: Detail the program model, curriculum, delivery methods, timeline, and milestones. HRSA reviewers assess feasibility and replicability; with only 3 employees and $180K annual revenue, you must show a credible operational plan and any partnerships that extend your capacity.
promptsForApplicant: Which specific trade skill(s) will this grant fund, and what is the instructional sequence (hours, modules, credentials)?
How will training be delivered — in-person, hybrid, employer-site? What facilities or equipment will be used?
What is the maximum cohort size per cycle, and how many cycles will run during the project period?
Which employers, community colleges, or workforce-development boards have committed (in writing) to partner with you, and what role does each play?
What is your month-by-month milestone plan for Year 1, and how does it scale if a second or third year is funded?
How will you recruit participants, and what wrap-around supports (childcare, transportation stipends, tools) will you provide?

sections.heading: Organizational Capability
sections.guidance: Demonstrate that a 3-person, 4-year-old nonprofit can manage federal funds and deliver at scale. Reviewers will scrutinize whether your infrastructure — financial controls, data systems, staffing — is commensurate with the award size. Your disadvantaged status may be a scoring factor if the NOFO includes such preferences.
promptsForApplicant: Who on your team (or contracted) will serve as Project Director and Grants/Fiscal Manager, and what are their relevant credentials?
Do you currently hold any federal or state grants, and if so, what was your compliance record (audits, reporting)?
What accounting software and internal controls do you use to segregate and track grant expenditures?
Can you produce audited financials or a reviewed financial statement for the most recent fiscal year?
What is your board's role in financial oversight, and how many active board members do you have?
How does your organization's disadvantaged status (as documented) align with any HRSA equity or priority-population preferences stated in the NOFO?

sections.heading: Budget Narrative
sections.guidance: Justify every line item as allowable, allocable, and reasonable under 45 CFR Part 75 (HHS cost principles). With a $180K annual operating budget, reviewers will scrutinize whether requested federal funds supplant existing revenue or genuinely expand capacity. Indirect cost rate must be disclosed.
promptsForApplicant: What personnel costs (salaries, FTEs, fringe) will be charged to the grant, and are those rates consistent with your current payroll?
What is your negotiated indirect cost rate or NICRA, and if you lack one, will you claim the 10% de minimis rate?
What equipment or supplies are essential to the project, and can you justify why they cannot be borrowed or rented?
If you plan to subcontract any program delivery, who is the subcontractor, what is the scope, and how was the cost determined?
How will you demonstrate that grant funds expand services rather than replace your existing $180K operating budget?
What is your cost-per-participant trained, and how does it compare to similar programs regionally?

sections.heading: Outcomes & Evaluation
sections.guidance: HRSA grants require measurable performance metrics tied to the project's logic model. Describe how you will collect, analyze, and report data on outputs, outcomes, and long-term impact. With limited staff, the data-collection plan must be realistic.
promptsForApplicant: What are your three to five primary performance measures (e.g., enrollment, completion rate, credential attainment, 90-day job placement rate)?
What data systems — even simple spreadsheet-based — will you use to track participant progress from intake through post-program follow-up?
How will you conduct participant follow-up at 90 days and 1 year post-completion, and who is responsible?
Will you use an external evaluator, and if not, how will you ensure objectivity in self-reported outcomes?
What is your baseline data for each metric so reviewers can assess the magnitude of change you are targeting?

watchOuts: CRITICAL: The NOFO number HRSA-26-036 was not accompanied by any description or eligibility text. Before investing further effort, locate the official NOFO on grants.gov or HRSA.gov and confirm (a) eligible applicant types, (b) whether nonprofits qualify without a health-workforce or HRSA-specific mandate, and (c) the actual submission deadline and funding ceiling.

HRSA grants are predominantly health-workforce focused; a trade-skills nonprofit must explicitly connect its training to HRSA priority areas (e.g., healthcare support occupations, behavioral health paraprofessionals) or it may be deemed ineligible at the technical review stage.

With only 3 employees, reviewers may question organizational capacity for federal award management; plan to address this directly or risk a 'high risk' designation that triggers additional reporting burdens.

Your annual revenue of $180K means even a modest federal award could exceed your current operating budget; be prepared to demonstrate financial management capacity or the application may be scored down on organizational capability.

Disadvantaged status alone (without woman-owned, veteran-owned, or minority-owned designations) may not trigger specific HRSA scoring preferences — verify whether the NOFO defines 'disadvantaged' in a way that aligns with your certification.
Verify your SAM.gov registration is active and your UEI is in good standing before submission; a lapsed registration is an automatic disqualifier for any federal award.
If the NOFO requires a matching or cost-share commitment, your current revenue base may make a meaningful match difficult to document — check this requirement immediately.

Vermont nonprofits with less than $750K in federal expenditures in a fiscal year are below the Single Audit threshold, but you should still confirm whether the NOFO imposes any additional audit or financial-review requirements.

candidates.title: Regional Alliances and Multistakeholder Partnerships to Stimulate (RAMPS) Cybersecurity Education and Workforce Development
candidates.agencyName: National Institute of Standards and Technology
candidates.rationale: Cybersecurity education and workforce development theme is thematically relevant, but RAMPS awards are typically structured for regional nonprofit/educational consortia, not for-profit consultancies.

candidates.title: Small Business Innovation Research (SBIR) program, Phase I
candidates.rationale: SBIR Phase I is open to small for-profit businesses and the applicant qualifies by size, but the disability/rehabilitation R&D focus of NIDILRR has no alignment with cybersecurity services.

candidates.title: Small Business Innovation Research Program (SBIR) Phase II
candidates.rationale: SBIR Phase II requires a prior Phase I award from NIDILRR and is disability-research focused, making both the eligibility pathway and topic area mismatched.

candidates.title: CyberAICorps Scholarship for Service
candidates.rationale: CyberAICorps is a scholarship-for-service program targeting individual students, not small business grant applicants.

reasons: No eligibility text was provided for opportunity 25-531; it is impossible to assess whether the applicant meets any stated criteria.
The applicant type field is listed as '(unspecified)', so no entity-type restrictions can be evaluated.
The funding range and agency are both unknown, preventing assessment of fit or scope.
blockers: No UEI/SAM.gov registration found in applicant profile. Active SAM.gov registration with a valid UEI is a universal prerequisite for federal grant awards and is currently absent.
result.notes: Critical next steps: (1) Complete or verify SAM.gov registration and obtain a valid UEI before any federal grant application can proceed. (2) Retrieve the full opportunity announcement (FOA/NOFO) for 25-531 to obtain actual eligibility criteria, applicant type restrictions, funding range, and agency requirements. (3) Once eligibility text is available, re-assess against applicant attributes: NAICS 541512 (Custom Computer Programming Services), 8 employees, $1.2M revenue, for-profit, 3 years in operation, Texas-based, woman-owned. These details could be relevant to size standards, geographic restrictions, or set-aside provisions that cannot be evaluated without the opportunity text.

reasons: No eligibility text was provided for opportunity 2026-NIST-RAMPS-01; it is impossible to confirm or deny whether the applicant meets stated eligibility criteria.
Applicant type restrictions (e.g., for-profit, non-profit, small business, academic) are unspecified — the opportunity lists 'applicant types accepted: unspecified'.
Funding range is not disclosed, so fit cannot be assessed against applicant size or project scope.
Agency is listed as 'unknown', preventing cross-reference with agency-specific eligibility rules (e.g., NIST RAMPS programs may have specific participant requirements that cannot be verified here).
blockers: No UEI/SAM.gov registration found in applicant profile. Active SAM.gov registration with a valid UEI is a universal requirement for federal grant recipients under 2 CFR 25. This is a hard blocker until resolved.
result.notes: The applicant's NAICS code (541512 - Computer Systems Design Services) and mission (cybersecurity assessments) are plausibly aligned with a NIST RAMPS-type cybersecurity program, but this cannot substitute for actual eligibility text. Recommended next steps: (1) Obtain and register a UEI on SAM.gov immediately — this is required before any federal award can be made; (2) Retrieve the full opportunity announcement from grants.gov or NIST to review eligibility criteria, cost-sharing requirements, and NAICS code restrictions; (3) Confirm whether the opportunity accepts for-profit entities, as many NIST grant programs restrict eligibility to academic institutions or non-profits; (4) Verify whether woman-owned status qualifies for any set-aside or priority scoring under this opportunity.

steps.message: Drafting for 25-531 — best uncertain match in shortlist.

result.summary: Insufficient grant data was provided to generate a tailored proposal skeleton. No agency, funding range, award count, close date, description, or eligibility text is available for Opportunity 25-531. The skeleton below uses a generic federal small-business grant structure as a stand-in, but the applicant must locate and supply the actual solicitation before relying on this framework.

sections.heading: Statement of Need
sections.guidance: Establish the market problem or gap that the project addresses. Without the official description, ground this in documented cybersecurity risk trends affecting mid-market firms and any federal policy priorities (e.g., NIST CSF, CISA advisories) that signal demand for the proposed work.
promptsForApplicant: What specific cybersecurity gap or threat trend — supported by data — does your work directly address for mid-market clients?
Which federal, state, or industry reports can you cite to substantiate that this need is urgent and underserved in Texas and/or nationally?
Why is an 8-person, woman-owned firm with 3 years of focused IR and assessment experience uniquely positioned to address this need versus larger incumbents?

Does the awarding agency have a stated strategic priority (e.g., critical infrastructure protection, SMB resilience) that directly maps to your mission? Identify it once you retrieve the full solicitation.

sections.heading: Project Approach
sections.guidance: Describe the technical and operational methodology. Without the solicitation's scope of work, keep this section placeholder-driven — you must align every task to the funding agency's stated objectives once retrieved.
promptsForApplicant: What specific services, tools, or methodologies will be delivered under this award (e.g., penetration testing frameworks, incident-response playbooks, tabletop exercises)?
What is your phased work plan, and what are the deliverables and completion dates for each phase?
How does your approach comply with recognized standards such as NIST SP 800-61, ISO 27001, or SOC 2 Type II?
What sub-contractors or teaming partners, if any, will support delivery, and how will you manage and oversee them?
How will you ensure continuity of service given your current 8-person headcount if award scope exceeds current capacity?

sections.heading: Organizational Capability
sections.guidance: Demonstrate that the firm has the past performance, personnel, and infrastructure to execute. Highlight woman-owned small business (WOSB) status as a differentiator if the solicitation set-asides or preferences align.
promptsForApplicant: What are the three most relevant past projects (client type, scope, dollar value, outcome) that demonstrate your ability to perform work of similar size and complexity?
List key personnel, their certifications (CISSP, CEH, GCIH, etc.), and their specific roles in this project.
Is your firm registered in SAM.gov with an active, unexpired registration, and is your WOSB certification current in the SBA's WOSB Program Repository?
Do you hold any GSA Schedule contracts, agency-specific BPAs, or other federal vehicles that demonstrate prior federal contracting experience?
What quality-control or project-management processes do you have in place to ensure on-time, on-budget delivery?

sections.heading: Budget Narrative
sections.guidance: Provide a line-item justification for all costs. Without knowing the funding range, build a scalable model with clearly separated direct and indirect costs. Federal auditors scrutinize small firms on indirect rate substantiation.
promptsForApplicant: What are your fully-loaded labor rates for each role (title, hours, rate, basis), and how were those rates established?
What fringe benefit and indirect cost rates do you apply, and are they DCAA-negotiated or internally calculated?
What direct materials, software licenses, travel, or other direct costs are required, and how is each tied to a specific deliverable?
If the solicitation has a cost-share or matching requirement, how will you document and fund your share?
What is your profit/fee percentage, and how does it compare to agency norms for this type of work?

sections.heading: Outcomes & Evaluation
sections.guidance: Define measurable success metrics and a monitoring plan. Federal agencies increasingly require logic models or performance measurement frameworks tied to program goals — which cannot be fully specified until the solicitation is retrieved.
promptsForApplicant: What are three to five quantifiable outputs and outcomes you will deliver (e.g., number of assessments completed, reduction in mean-time-to-detect for client engagements)?
How will you collect, track, and report performance data to the awarding agency, and at what frequency?
What is your plan if a key milestone is missed — how will you notify the contracting officer and remediate?
Does the solicitation specify a required evaluation methodology or reporting template? If so, identify it once you retrieve the full document.
How will the results of this award extend beyond the period of performance — what is the sustainability or commercialization path?

watchOuts: CRITICAL: The solicitation (25-531) contains no description, eligibility text, agency name, funding range, award count, or close date. Do NOT submit anything based on this skeleton alone — retrieve the full solicitation from SAM.gov or the issuing agency portal before proceeding.

Eligibility is completely unknown. Confirm whether this is restricted to nonprofits, academic institutions, or specific NAICS codes before investing proposal effort — NAICS 541512 may or may not be eligible.

If the opportunity is an SBIR/STTR, for-profit status is acceptable, but the 3-year age and $1.2M revenue must be checked against the SBA small business size standard for NAICS 541512 (currently $34M average annual receipts) — you currently qualify, but verify the applicable table.

WOSB set-aside preference only applies if (a) the solicitation designates a WOSB or EDWOSB set-aside and (b) your WOSB certification is active in the SBA repository. Do not assume the designation applies.

With only 3 years in operation, past performance citations may be thin for competitive federal awards that require 5+ years of directly relevant experience — identify any teaming or mentor-protégé options early.
SAM.gov registration must be active and not expired at time of submission; registration lapses are a frequent, automatic disqualifier for small firms.
Without a negotiated indirect cost rate agreement (NICRA), some agencies will cap indirect costs or require a de minimis rate — determine the agency's policy before finalizing the budget.
If award involves access to federal systems or CUI, personnel may need background investigations or clearances; assess clearance timelines against any required period-of-performance start date.

summary.intent: My woman-owned cybersecurity consultancy in Texas has 8 employees and $1.2M revenue. Any SBA innovation grants?
